title.rendered: I’ve Got a Feeling (Everything’s Gonna Be Alright)

content.rendered:
I had this post originally scheduled for the end of the month, but it struck me this morning as better suited for today.

On the evening of December6 2001, Bruce kicked off his holiday concert by joining the South Community Choir of Asbury Park for an eight-minute gospel rave-up of “I’ve Got a Feeling (Everything’s Gonna Be Alright).”

It was such a spirit-lifter for a community still remembering how to celebrate so soon after a devastating tragedy that Bruce went on to perform it with them five more times over the next two years.

And while it’s been almost 17 years since he’s last performed it, today seems like a good day for a little reminder that while things sometimes take a little while to clarify, I’ve got a feeling everything’s gonna be alright.

I’ve Got a Feeling (Everything’s Gonna Be Alright)
First performed: December 6, 2001 (Asbury Park, NJ)
Last performed: December 8, 2003 (Asbury Park, NJ)

© November 4, 2020

excerpt.rendered: In 2001 and 2003, Bruce opened several of his Asbury Park holiday shows by joining the South Community Choir of Asbury Park on this timely gospel classic.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Changing Children
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: At the dawn of the 1970s, even Bruce couldn't help but be a little cynical about the likelihood of real change. Take a listen to his sarcastic Steel Mill song, "Change It."

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Lonesome Day
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Relationship song or 9/11 catharsis? Either way, "Lonesome Day" helps us process the pain of betrayal and hold our self-destructive urges in check.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: In Michigan
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: In a bolt of pre-show inspiration, Bruce wrote one of his all-time greatest songs for an appreciative Michigan audience.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Why’d You Do That
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One of the slightest songs in Bruce's catalog, "Why'd You Do That" at least provides the framework for some epic jams.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Mississippi
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Long before "The Ghost of Tom Joad," nineteen-year-old Bruce Springsteen adapted another great work of literature into song.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Don’t Say No
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: We may not be able to make out the lyrics for "Don't Say No," but the backing track is a keeper. Too bad Bruce didn't find a home for it--although it's not for lack of trying.
